text:
The effect of different solvent systems on the extraction of antioxidants from black mulberry (Morus nigra), blackberry (Rubus ulmifolius) and strawberry (Fragaria x ananassa) was investigated in this study. After evaluating the effectiveness of different extraction methods through quantification of total phenolic content, anthocyanin and overall antioxidant activity of the extracts, the influence of the solvent systems was further clarified through principal component analysis (PCA).

The best solvent composition for the extraction of selected phenolic compounds (by percentage of recovery for each compound and for each berry type) was acetone/water 70/30 (v/v), whereas the best composition for the extraction of anthocyanins was methanol/water/acetic acid 70/29.5/0.5 (v/v/v). The optimal composition of the solvent mixtures for antioxidant capacity was different for each fruit: ethanol/water 50/50 (v/v) for black mulberries, acetone/water 70/29.5/v/v blackberries and acetone/water 50/50 (v/v) for strawberries. Generally, the antioxidant capacity of extracts is highly dependent on the solvent composition used for extraction. PCA analysis shows that all the aqueous-organic mixtures used for extraction were sufficient to discriminate between extracts obtained using pure organic solvents. Hence, the presence of water in the organic-based mixtures is crucial for the optimization of the phytochemical extraction.
explanation: The passage is very polished and reads like an abstract with precise solvent ratios and PCA mentions, but contains a small inconsistency ('70/29.5/v/v') that a human author would likely catch, suggesting AI assistance.
